content:
1	# Second Brain Wiki Configuration
2	
3	This file configures the skill for Evan's environment. Edit paths and
4	projects as your work evolves.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Paths
9	
10	| Path | Value | Description |
11	|------|-------|-------------|
12	| Wiki directory | `~/second-brain/wiki/` | Where wiki pages live |
13	| Sources directory | `~/second-brain/sources/` | Raw source files stored here |
14	| Inbox directory | `~/Desktop/Second Brain Inbox/` | Drop files here for processing |
15	
16	---
17	
18	## Active Projects
19	
20	These are your current projects and areas of focus. The skill uses these
21	to find connections when ingesting new content.
22	
23	- **evaluator-super-agent** — automated prototype quality assessment using persona-driven walkthroughs, 7-dimension rubric scoring, actor/evaluator separation
24	- **prototype-creator** — generates clickable prototypes from RFEs, tiered AC classification, Playwright automation
25	- **agent-eval-harness** — framework for evaluating AI skill quality, eval.yaml configs, judges, scoring pipelines
26	- **ai-sdlc-pipeline** — three-phase pipeline: RFE Creator (WHAT) → Prototype Creator (SHOW) → Strat Creator (HOW)
27	- **my-cursor-claw** — personal workspace with 20+ skills, MCP connections, notetaking, automations
28	- **decision-kit** — Decision Driven Development toolkit, /strategize, /decide, /journal
29	- **automated-usability-testing** — simulated usability testing tool, persona YAML files, evaluate-flow protocol
30	- **archie** — UX research agent for the RHOAIUX team
31	- **rhoai-prototypes** — RHOAI prototype collection for evaluation
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Calendar Integration
36	
37	| Setting | Value |
38	|---------|-------|
39	| Calendar name | `primary` |
40	| Auto-ingest meetings | `true` |
41	| Minimum meeting duration | `15 min` |
42	| Require notes/transcript | `true` |
43	| Skip recurring without notes | `true` |
44	
45	Events that qualify for auto-ingestion:
46	- Have attached meeting notes or a description with substance (not just "Weekly sync")
47	- Have a linked Google Doc (agenda, transcript, action items)
48	- Duration is 15+ minutes
49	- Are not recurring meetings that had no notes added
50	
51	---
52	
53	## MCP Integrations
54	
55	| Integration | MCP Server | Used for |
56	|-------------|-----------|----------|
57	| Google Docs | `user-google-workspace` | Extracting doc content |
58	| Google Calendar | `user-google-workspace` | Meeting ingestion |
59	| Slack | `project-0-my-cursor-claw-slack` | Channel message ingestion |
60	
61	---
62	
63	## Cross-Link Priority
64	
65	When multiple connections exist, prioritize these categories (from the
66	wiki index):
67	
68	1. **Red Hat AI-SDLC** — pipeline components, RFE/prototype/strat flow
69	2. **Evaluation & Quality** — rubrics, heuristics, eval harness
70	3. **Tools & Skills** — decision kit, cursor claw, skill overlays
71	4. **Meetings** — meeting notes with action items and decisions
72	5. **Standups** — sprint progress and blockers
73	6. **Session Logs** — what was built and shipped
74

# Prototype Creator

1	# Wiki Page Template
2	
3	Use this template when creating new wiki pages. Copy the structure,
4	fill in the bracketed fields.
5	
6	---
7	
8	## Template
9	
10	```markdown
11	# <Page Title>
12	
13	<One-sentence summary of what this page covers.>
14	
15	## Key Points
16	
17	- <Bullet point 1: a concrete fact, concept, or takeaway>
18	- <Bullet point 2>
19	- <Bullet point 3>
20	- <Additional bullets as needed, max 10>
21	
22	## Related
23	
24	- [<Related Page Title>](<related-slug>.md) — <one-line reason for connection>
25	- [<Another Related Page>](<slug>.md) — <connection reason>
26	
27	## Sources
28	
29	- <path/to/source/file or URL where this came from>
30	- Ingested: <YYYY-MM-DD>
31	```
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Good Example
36	
37	```markdown
38	# Retrieval Augmented Generation
39	
40	Technique that grounds LLM responses in retrieved documents rather than
41	relying solely on training data.
42	
43	## Key Points
44	
45	- Combines a retriever (vector search) with a generator (LLM)
46	- Reduces hallucination by providing source documents as context
47	- Trade-off: retrieval quality limits generation quality
48	- Common stack: embeddings → vector DB → reranker → LLM prompt
49	- Works best when source corpus is well-chunked and up-to-date
50	
51	## Related
52	
53	- [Vector Databases](vector-databases.md) — the retrieval layer in RAG pipelines
54	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — uses embedding similarity for judge calibration
55	
56	## Sources
57	
58	- ~/second-brain/sources/what-is-rag.md
59	- Ingested: 2026-06-10
60	```
61	
62	---
63	
64	## Bad Example
65	
66	```markdown
67	# RAG
68	
69	Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique in artificial intelligence
70	that combines information retrieval with text generation. It was first proposed
71	by Lewis et al. in 2020. The basic idea is that instead of relying solely on
72	what a language model learned during training, you can augment it with relevant
73	documents retrieved at inference time.
74	
75	This approach has several advantages. First, it reduces hallucination because
76	the model can reference actual documents. Second, it allows the model to access
77	up-to-date information that wasn't in its training data. Third, it provides
78	transparency because you can trace responses back to source documents.
79	
80	There are several implementations of RAG available today...
81	```
82	
83	**Why it's bad:**
84	- Reads like a Wikipedia article, not a wiki node
85	- No bullet summaries (walls of prose)
86	- No Related section (isolated page, no connections)
87	- No Sources section (where did this come from?)
88	- Title is an acronym without expansion
89	- Contains filler ("There are several..." "This approach has...")
90

text: There's already a page called `nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md` — I need to check its content for overlap. Let me read it and try fetching the article via curl in parallel.

content:
1	# Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge
2	
3	First hands-on internship assignment: apply Nielsen Norman's 10 Usability Heuristics to Kubernetes-style interfaces across multiple AI tools and compare their analysis quality.
4	
5	## The Jagged Edge
6	
7	Research from Harvard Business School shows AI capabilities aren't uniform — models are very good at some tasks and surprisingly bad at others. The boundary between "good" and "bad" isn't obvious. This is the "jagged technological frontier."
8	
9	## What Was Tested
10	
11	- Selected Kubernetes/OpenShift UI screens at different complexity levels
12	- Crafted evaluation prompts asking each tool to assess against all 10 heuristics with severity ratings (1–4)
13	- Ran the same prompt through Cursor, Claude Code, Gemini (Flash + Pro), and ChatGPT
14	
15	## Comparison Dimensions
16	
17	- Quality of analysis — did it correctly identify real usability issues?
18	- Depth — surface-level observations vs. genuine insight?
19	- Specificity — generic advice vs. grounded in the actual interface?
20	- Structure — how well-organized and actionable?
21	- Hallucinations — did it make things up?
22	- Unique insights — did any tool catch something others missed?
23	
24	## Deliverables
25	
26	- [Jaquez - Task 1 - Nielsen & Agents](https://docs.google.com/document/d/11o9Px8wddeal3Ap7vBVtyDSw8FWaMGsuTYQwyc0hSAQ) (Google Doc)
27	- [Update — Evan Jaquez](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1AoUZtCCJf4btr68jOY3_xKbJU2wyx6XTqjGn6IIRd3w) (Google Slides)
28	
29	## Relevance to Current Work
30	
31	The comparison methodology directly informs the [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — understanding which AI tools produce research-quality heuristic evaluations vs. shallow summaries is critical for choosing the right approach to automated prototype evaluation.
32	
33	## Related
34	
35	- [UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md) — rubric-based scoring approach
36	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — applies these learnings
37	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — where evaluations will be used
38	- [Ansible AI Helpers Strategy — 2026-06-17](ansible-ai-helpers-strategy-2026-06-17.md) — heuristic eval skill selected as Ansible pilot
39	- [Standup — June 29](standup-2026-06-29.md) — usability comparison approach being iterated on
40	- [RH AI-First UX — June 25](rh-ai-first-ux-2026-06-25.md) — unified testing strategy connects to heuristic methodology
41	- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — team agreed to adopt Nielsen's heuristics as base rubric for evaluations
42	
43	## Sources
44	
45	- sources/2026-06-03-nielsen-agents-task1.html
46

input.command:
curl -s "https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics/" | python3 -c "
import sys
import re

html = sys.stdin.read()

# Remove script and style tags
html = re.sub(r'<script[^>]*>.*?</script>', '', html, flags=re.DOTALL)
html = re.sub(r'<style[^>]*>.*?</style>', '', html, flags=re.DOTALL)

# Extract main article content (look for article body)
article_match = re.search(r'<article[^>]*>(.*?)</article>', html, re.DOTALL)
if article_match:
    content = article_match.group(1)
else:
    content = html

# Remove all HTML tags
text = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', '', content)

# Clean up whitespace
text = re.sub(r'\n{3,}', '\n\n', text)
text = re.sub(r'  +', ' ', text)
lines = [l.strip() for l in text.split('\n') if l.strip()]

# Print first 200 non-empty lines
for i, line in enumerate(lines[:200]):
    print(line)
" 2>&1 | head -200
input.description: Fetch and extract text content from Nielsen Norman usability heuristics article

content:
7
10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design
Jakob Nielsen
Jakob Nielsen
April 24, 1994 · Updated Jan. 30, 2024
2024-01-30
Share
Email article
Share on LinkedIn
Share on Twitter
Summary: 
Jakob Nielsen's 10 general principles for interaction design. They are called "heuristics" because they are broad rules of thumb and not specific usability guidelines.
Thank you to Kelley Gordon for designing the visuals and posters included in this article.
Thank you to Kate Moran and Feifei Liu for updating the heuristic names, descriptions, and examples.
In This Article:
1: Visibility of System Status
2: Match Between the System and the Real World
3: User Control and Freedom
4: Consistency and Standards
5: Error Prevention
6: Recognition Rather than Recall
7: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use
8: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design
9: Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors
10: Help and Documentation
Free 10 Heuristics Poster
1: Visibility of System Status
The design should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within a reasonable amount of time.
When users know the current system status, they learn the outcome of their prior interactions and determine next steps. Predictable interactions create trust in the product as well as the brand.
Go one step further than definitions.
Use the heuristics to defend and inform your design in our self-paced course, 10 Usability Heuristics — created by the team who brought you the framework first.
What You'll Learn
Example of Usability Heuristic #1:
You Are Here indicators on mall maps show people where they currently are, to help them understand where to go next.
Tips
Communicate clearly to users what the system’s state is — no action with consequences to users should be taken without informing them.
Present feedback to the user as quickly as possible (ideally, immediately).
Build trust through open and continuous communication.
Learn more
Full article: Visibility of System Status
3-minute video about the Visibility Heuristic
2: Match Between the System and the Real World
The design should speak the users' language. Use words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user, rather than internal jargon. Follow real-world conventions, making information appear in a natural and logical order.
The way you should design depends very much on your specific users. Terms, concepts, icons, and images that seem perfectly clear to you and your colleagues may be unfamiliar or confusing to your users.
When a design’s controls follow real-world conventions and correspond to desired outcomes (called natural mapping), it’s easier for users to learn and remember how the interface works. This helps to build an experience that feels intuitive.
Example of Usability Heuristic #2:
When stovetop controls match the layout of heating elements, users can quickly understand which control maps to which heating element.
Tips
Ensure that users can understand meaning without having to go look up a word’s definition.
Never assume your understanding of words or concepts will match that of your users.
User research will uncover your users' familiar terminology, as well as their mental models around important concepts.
Learn more
Full article: Match Between the System and the Real World
2-minute video: Match Between the System and the Real World
3: User Control and Freedom
Users often perform actions by mistake. They need a clearly marked "emergency exit" to leave the unwanted action without having to go through an extended process.
When it's easy for people to back out of a process or undo an action, it fosters a sense of freedom and confidence. Exits allow users to remain in control of the system and avoid getting stuck and feeling frustrated.
Example of Usability Heuristic #3:
Digital spaces need quick emergency exits, just like physical spaces do.
Tips
Support Undo and Redo.
Show a clear way to exit the current interaction, like a Cancel button.
Make sure the exit is clearly labeled and discoverable.
Learn more
Full article: User Control and Freedom
2-minute video: User Control and Freedom
4: Consistency and Standards
Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing. Follow platform and industry conventions.
Jakob's Law states that people spend most of their time using digital products other than yours. Users’ experiences with those other products set their expectations. Failing to maintain consistency may increase the users' cognitive load by forcing them to learn something new.
Example of Usability Heuristic #4:
Checkin counters are usually located at the front of hotels. This consistency meets customers’ expectations.
Tips
Improve learnability by maintaining both types of consistency: internal and external.
Maintain consistency within a single product or a family of products (internal consistency).
Follow established industry conventions (external consistency).
Learn more
Full article: Consistency and Standards
3-minute video: Consistency and Standards
5: Error Prevention
Good error messages are important, but the best designs carefully prevent problems from occurring in the first place. Either eliminate error-prone conditions, or check for them and present users with a confirmation option before they commit to the action.
There are two types of errors: slips and mistakes. Slips are unconscious errors caused by inattention. Mistakes are conscious errors based on a mismatch between the user’s mental model and the design.
Example of Usability Heuristic #5:
​​​​Guard rails on curvy mountain roads prevent drivers from falling off cliffs.
Tips
Prioritize your effort: Prevent high-cost errors first, then little frustrations.
Avoid slips by providing helpful constraints and good defaults.
Prevent mistakes by removing memory burdens, supporting undo, and warning your users.
Learn more
Full article: Preventing User Errors
3-minute video: Error Prevention
6: Recognition Rather than Recall
Minimize the user's memory load by making elements, actions, and options visible. The user should not have to remember information from one part of the interface to another. Information required to use the design (e.g. field labels or menu items) should be visible or easily retrievable when needed.
Humans have limited short-term memories. Interfaces that promote recognition reduce the amount of cognitive effort required from users.
Example of Usability Heuristic #6:
It’s easier for most people to recognize the capitals of countries, instead of having to remember them. People are more likely to correctly answer the question Is Lisbon the capital of Portugal? rather than What’s the capital of Portugal?
Tips
Let people recognize information in the interface, rather than forcing them to remember (“recall”) it.
Offer help in context, instead of giving users a long tutorial to memorize.
Reduce the information that users have to remember.
Learn more
Full article: Recognition vs. Recall in UX
3-minute video: Recognition vs. Recall
7: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use
Shortcuts — hidden from novice users — may speed up the interaction for the expert user so that the design can cater to both inexperienced and experienced users. Allow users to tailor frequent actions.
Flexible processes can be carried out in different ways, so that people can pick whichever method works for them.
Example of Usability Heuristic #7:
Regular routes are listed on maps, but locals with knowledge of the area can take shortcuts.
Tips
Provide accelerators like keyboard shortcuts and touch gestures.
Provide personalization by tailoring content and functionality for individual users.
Allow for customization, so users can make selections about how they want the product to work.
Learn more
Full article: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use: The 7th Usability Heuristic Explained
3-minute video: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use
8: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design
Interfaces should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed. Every extra unit of information in an interface competes with the relevant units of information and diminishes their relative visibility.
This heuristic doesn't mean you have to use a flat design — it's about making sure you're keeping the content and visual design focused on the essentials. Ensure that the visual elements of the interface support the user's primary goals.
Example of Usability Heuristic #8:
​​​​​​An ornate teapot may have excessive decorative elements, like an uncomfortable handle or hard-to-wash nozzle, that can interfere with usability.
Tips
Keep the content and visual design of UI focused on the essentials.
Don't let unnecessary elements distract users from the information they really need.
Prioritize the content and features to support primary goals.
Learn more
Full article: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design (Usability Heuristic #8)
3-minute video: Aesthetic and Minimalist Design
9: Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, and Recover from Errors
Error messages should be expressed in plain language (no error codes), precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution.
These error messages should also be presented with visual treatments that will help users notice and recognize them.
Example of Usability Heuristic #9:
Wrong way signs on the road remind drivers that they are heading in the wrong direction and ask them to stop.
Tips
Use traditional error-message visuals, like bold, red text.
Tell users what went wrong in language they will understand — avoid technical jargon.
Offer users a solution, like a shortcut that can solve the error immediately.
Learn more
Full article: Error-Message Guidelines
2-minute video: Helping Users Overcome Errors
10: Help and Documentation
It’s best if the system doesn’t need any additional explanation. However, it may be necessary to provide documentation to help users understand how to complete their tasks.
Help and documentation content should be easy to search and focused on the user's task. Keep it concise, and list concrete steps that need to be carried out.
Example of Usability Heuristic #10:
Information kiosks at airports are easily recognizable and solve customers’ problems in context and immediately.
Tips
Ensure that the help documentation is easy to search.
Whenever possible, present the documentation in context right at the moment that the user requires it.
List concrete steps to be carried out.
Learn more
Full article: Help and Documentation: The 10th Usability Heuristic
3-minute video: Help and Documentation
Free 10 Heuristics Poster
Download a free summary poster or 10 detailed posters of the 10 Usability Heuristics, designed by Kelley Gordon, Kate Moran, and Feifei Liu.
Download and print our free usability heuristic posters. Hang them at home, in your office, or gift them to a colleague.
Note from Jakob
I originally developed the heuristics for heuristic evaluation in collaboration with Rolf Molich in 1990 [Molich and Nielsen 1990; Nielsen and Molich 1990]. Four years later, I refined the heuristics based on a factor analysis of 249 usability problems [Nielsen 1994a] to derive a set of heuristics with maximum explanatory power, resulting in this revised set of heuristics [Nielsen 1994b].
In 2020, we updated this article, adding more explanation, examples, and related links. While we slightly refined the language of the definitions, the 10 heuristics themselves have remained relevant and unchanged since 1994. When something has remained true for 26 years, it will likely apply to future generations of user interfaces as well.
See Also
Examples
10 Usability Heuristics Applied to Complex Applications — Examples of the heuristics applied to complex and domain-specific software applications.
10 Usability Heuristics Applied to Virtual Reality — See the heuristics applied to 3D virtual environments.
10 Usability Heuristics Applied to Video Games — Great examples of the 10 heuristics in highly interactive and highly visual user interfaces that have an entertainment purpose.
10 Usability Heuristics Applied to Everyday Life (Just for fun)
Checklists & Guidelines
Full set of 2,397 UX design guidelines (across multiple reports).
Bruce "Tog" Tognazzini's list of basic principles for interface design. The list is slightly too long for heuristic evaluation but serves as a useful checklist.
References
Molich, R., and Nielsen, J. (1990). Improving a human-computer dialogue, Communications of the ACM 33, 3 (March), 338-348.
Nielsen, J., and Molich, R. (1990). Heuristic evaluation of user interfaces, Proc. ACM CHI'90 Conf. (Seattle, WA, 1-5 April), 249-256.
Nielsen, J. (1994a). Enhancing the explanatory power of usability heuristics. Proc. ACM CHI'94 Conf. (Boston, MA, April 24-28), 152-158.
Nielsen, J. (1994b). Heuristic evaluation. In Nielsen, J., and Mack, R.L. (Eds.), Usability Inspection Methods, John Wiley & Sons, New York, NY.
Citing the Heuristics
You may use these heuristics in your own work. Please credit Jakob Nielsen and provide the address for this page [nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics] or cite the paper above [Nielsen 1994a]. If you want to print copies of this page or reproduce the content online, however, please see our copyright info for details. Copyright © by Jakob Nielsen.ISSN 1548-5552
Free Downloads
All Posters - 10 Usability Heuristics (ZIP)
Summary Poster - 10 Usability Heuristics (PDF)
Summary Poster, A4 Size - 10 Usability Heuristics (PDF)
Summary Poster, Letter Size - 10 Usability Heuristics (PDF)
Related Courses
UX Basic Training
Foundational concepts that everyone should know
Interaction
ten heuristics,Heuristic Evaluation,Top 10
Related Topics
Heuristic Evaluation
Heuristic Evaluation

1	# UX Quality Rubric
2	
3	The scoring system used by [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) to evaluate generated prototypes. Scored by a restricted `prototype-scorer` agent (no shell, no network, no MCP).
4	
5	## Dimensions (0–2 each, 8 total)
6	
7	| Dimension | What It Measures |
8	|---|---|
9	| **Completeness** | Does the prototype cover the RFE's user stories and acceptance criteria? |
10	| **Usability** | Is the interaction pattern clear and free of obvious friction? (Nielsen's 10 heuristics) |
11	| **Feasibility** | Can this be built with the target design system (PatternFly 6)? |
12	| **Fidelity Match** | Does the prototype match the requested fidelity level — not over or under-engineered? |
13	
14	## Pass Threshold
15	
16	6+ total with no zeros. Prototypes scoring above get `prototype-creator-rubric-pass`. Below get `prototype-creator-needs-attention`.
17	
18	## Scoring Agent
19	
20	One invocation per dimension ensures scores are deterministic and auditable. The scorer agent is restricted — no shell, no network, no MCP — to prevent contamination.
21	
22	## Connection to Evaluator Super Agent
23	
24	The [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) will extend this rubric with additional dimensions from simulated usability and desirability testing. See also [Nielsen's Heuristics](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) for the research that informs the usability dimension.
25	
26	## Related
27	
28	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — the pipeline that uses this rubric
29	- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — extends this scoring
30	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — informs the usability dimension
31	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — similar pattern of rubric-based automated scoring
32	- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — scoring dimensions flagged for revisit at next meeting
33	
34	## Sources
35	
36	- sources/2026-06-09-prototype-creator-overview.html
37	- andybraren/prototype-creator config/ux-rubric.yaml
38

text: Overlap is LOW — the existing page is about an AI tool comparison experiment, not the heuristics themselves. I'll create a new canonical reference page and update related pages. Let me do these in parallel.

input.content:
# Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics

Foundational UX design principles by Jakob Nielsen (1994), refined from 249 usability problems. Used as the base rubric for heuristic evaluation and automated prototype assessment.

## Key Points

- **1 — Visibility of System Status**: Always inform users about what's happening; feedback should be timely and predictable — builds trust
- **2 — Match System to Real World**: Use language and concepts familiar to users, not internal jargon; follow real-world conventions and natural mapping
- **3 — User Control and Freedom**: Provide clear "emergency exits" (undo/redo, cancel); prevents users from feeling trapped by accidental actions
- **4 — Consistency and Standards**: Don't make users guess if different words/actions mean the same thing; follow platform conventions (Jakob's Law: users spend most time on other products)
- **5 — Error Prevention**: Prevent problems first; two error types — slips (unconscious, inattention) and mistakes (mental model mismatch); use constraints and good defaults
- **6 — Recognition Rather than Recall**: Make elements/actions/options visible; don't force users to remember info across interface sections; limited short-term memory
- **7 — Flexibility and Efficiency**: Provide accelerators (shortcuts, gestures) for experts while keeping the interface accessible to novices; allow customization
- **8 — Aesthetic and Minimalist Design**: Every extra info unit competes with relevant content; not about flat design — about keeping visual elements focused on user goals
- **9 — Help Recover from Errors**: Error messages in plain language, precisely identify the problem, constructively suggest a solution; use visual treatments to make errors noticeable
- **10 — Help and Documentation**: Best if no docs needed; when required — searchable, task-focused, concise, with concrete steps
- Originally developed with Rolf Molich (1990); refined via factor analysis of 249 problems (1994); unchanged since then
- Severity ratings 1–4 commonly used when applying heuristics to specific interfaces

## Related

- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — applied these heuristics in an AI model comparison across Cursor, Claude Code, Gemini, and ChatGPT
- [UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md) — usability dimension is grounded in these heuristics
- [Evaluator Super Agent](evaluator-super-agent.md) — automated prototype evaluation uses heuristics as scoring framework
- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — team adopted Nielsen's heuristics as base rubric for evaluations

## Sources

- https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ten-usability-heuristics/
- Ingested: 2026-07-09

1	# Evaluator Super Agent
2	
3	Evan's primary assignment: build an evaluation agent that automates prototype quality assessment within the [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) pipeline.
4	
5	**Status: Shipped.** The evaluator is a 3-phase skill pipeline on branch `evan/prototype-evaluate` at [andybraren/prototype-creator](https://github.com/andybraren/prototype-creator). It has evaluated 18+ STRATs against Jira acceptance criteria with Playwright persona walkthroughs.
6	
7	## What It Does (current)
8	
9	| Capability | Source | Status |
10	|---|---|---|
11	| AC evaluation | Custom Playwright persona journeys against Jira criteria | Shipped |
12	| Usability scoring | 7-dimension rubric with persona overlays (Zack Bodnar's framework) | Shipped |
13	| Consistency checking | Beau Morley's consistency checker (source + visual mode) | Shipped |
14	| Navigation reachability | Playwright verifies pages are clickable from sidebar | Shipped |
15	| Human review | Review tab in HTML report with override buttons + CSV writeback | Shipped |
16	| Report hosting | GitLab Pages (eval-reports-7ebc0e.pages.redhat.com) | Shipped |
17	| Google Sheet sync | Automated pass/fail + usability metrics to tracking sheet | Shipped |
18	| Feedback loop | Eval results → prototype-refine → re-eval (max 3 iterations) | In progress |
19	
20	## Architecture (confirmed June 16 with Andy)
21	
22	Evolved from "single monolithic super agent" to a **phased pipeline with overlays**:
23	
24	```
25	Phase 1: prototype-evaluate (extract ACs, RFE, Outcome, personas, journeys)
26	    ↓ writes extract-state.json
27	Phase 2: evaluate-journey (Playwright walkthroughs, consistency check)
28	    ↓ writes journey-log.json, screenshots, CSV
29	Phase 3: evaluate-usability (7-dimension persona scoring)
30	    ↓ updates journey-log.json
31	Phase 4: render-report.js + sync-sheet.js (HTML, CSV, Google Sheet)
32	```
33	
34	**Key design decisions:**
35	1. **Outcomes over Strategic Goals** as grounding criteria (Outcomes are consistently written)
36	2. **One Playwright pass** — shared screenshots across usability, consistency, and AC checks
37	3. **98% generic + 2% product overlay** (`config/product-overlay.yaml`) — portable to other products
38	4. **Hint mechanism** for dead-end navigation (failure logged but journey continues)
39	5. **Human-in-the-loop** — Review tab for designer input before feedback loop acts
40	
41	## Research References
42	
43	- **Avenir-UX** — autonomous UX evaluation agent using SEQ/SUS + persona framework
44	- **UXCascade** — multi-agent persona simulation with severity-rated issues (S1–S4)
45	- GitLab Notes REST API for MR comment posting
46	- Danger bot framework for CI-integrated review automation
47	
48	## How It Connects
49	
50	```
51	Jira STRAT (Outcome → RFE → STRAT)
52	    → prototype-create (generates code in workspace)
53	    → prototype-evaluate (this agent — 3 phases)
54	        ├── Phase 1: Extract ACs, personas, journeys from Jira
55	        ├── Phase 2: Playwright journeys + consistency check
56	        ├── Phase 3: Usability dimension scoring
57	        └── Phase 4: Report + Sheet sync
58	    → Human reviews report (override verdicts, add notes)
59	    → prototype-refine (acts on suggestions + human annotations)
60	    → Re-evaluate (loop until PASS or max iterations)
61	    → prototype-submit (MR to GitLab)
62	```
63	
64	## Key Design Inputs from Demo (May 14)
65	
66	From the [Prototype Creator Demo](prototype-creator-demo-may14.md):
67	- Consistency checkers should only evaluate what's NEW (CSS class="new" tagging)
68	- Even auto-mode should produce decision-kit pages for post-review
69	- Evaluator needs outcome-level awareness, not just feature-level
70	- Preview URL is the evaluation entry point (load the MR URL, assess in browser context)
71	- Token efficiency matters — scope evaluation to changed areas only
72	
73	## Related
74	
75	- [Outcome Tiger Team](outcome-tiger-team.md) — proposes outcome-level awareness for evaluation, directly extending this agent's scope
76	- [Prototype Creator Demo — May 14](prototype-creator-demo-may14.md) — walkthrough with design insights for this agent
77	- [Prototype Creator](prototype-creator.md) — the pipeline this plugs into
78	- [UX Quality Rubric](ux-quality-rubric.md) — existing scoring to extend
79	- [Agent Eval Harness](agent-eval-harness.md) — pattern for automated eval loops
80	- [Decision Kit](decision-kit.md) — using /strategize to plan this agent
81	- [Nielsen's Heuristics & The Jagged Edge](nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md) — informs methodology choice
82	- [My Cursor Claw](my-cursor-claw.md) — the workspace where this will be built
83	- [Ansible AI Helpers Strategy — 2026-06-17](ansible-ai-helpers-strategy-2026-06-17.md) — heuristic eval pilot connects to automated evaluation vision
84	
85	## Updates from June 16, 2026 Review
86	
87	Decisions made with Andy Braren (see [Andy/Evan Eval Review — June 16](andy-evan-eval-review-2026-06-16.md)):
88	
89	- **Architecture confirmed:** Centralized super evaluator, not fragmented skills. One Playwright pass, shared screenshots.
90	- **Grounding confirmed:** Outcomes as primary criteria, Strategic Goals excluded for now.
91	- **Overlay approach adopted:** 98% generic + 2% product-specific layer. See [GitLab skill-overlays best practice](https://gitlab.cee.redhat.com/global-engineering/wg-agentic-sdlc/-/blob/main/best-practices/skill-overlays.md).
92	- **Hint mechanism shipped:** Simulated UX researcher provides next URL on failure, allowing evaluation to continue while logging failure impact.
93	- **Screenshot consolidation:** Sub-agents share one set of Playwright screenshots instead of each capturing independently.
94	
95	- [RH AI-First UX — 2026-06-18](rh-ai-first-ux-2026-06-18.md) — Evan demoed automated usability testing + PatternFly consistency checker to full team
96	- [Andy/Evan Meeting — June 22](andy-evan-2026-06-22.md) — eval iteration history, flagging system, Prototype Creator integration strategy
97	- [AI-First Sync — June 25](ai-first-sync-2026-06-25.md) — Evan's full evaluator presentation to the team, readiness check discussion
98	- [RH AI-First UX — June 25](rh-ai-first-ux-2026-06-25.md) — testing fragmentation concerns, unified testing proposal
99	- [Standup — June 29](standup-2026-06-29.md) — 2 automated usability testing agents in development
100	- [Skill Overlays](skill-overlays.md) — context engineering technique relevant to team-specific overlays
101	- [Prompt Engineering Best Practices](prompt-engineering-best-practices.md) — sequential prompting and format-controlled output underpin the 3-phase evaluation pipeline
102	- [Andy/Evan Meeting — June 29](andy-evan-2026-06-29.md) — findings tab, auto-fix vs report-only modes, X-ray vs blind evaluation, centralized dashboard
103	- [Midpoint Check-in — June 30](midpoint-checkin-2026-06-30.md) — Zack: "pioneering work" combining research + AI + SDLC; report must be immediately actionable for designers
104	- [Weekly Design Sync — July 1](weekly-design-sync-2026-07-01.md) — Evan to demo evaluator next week; Nielsen's heuristics confirmed as base rubric
105	
106	## Sources
107	
108	- [Andy/Evan Eval Review — June 16](andy-evan-eval-review-2026-06-16.md)
109	- Andy/Evan meeting notes — 2026-06-08
110	- sources/2026-06-09-prototype-creator-overview.html
111	- /strategize session — 2026-06-09 (in progress)

text:
Done. Here's what was added:

**Created:** `nielsens-10-usability-heuristics.md` — canonical reference for all 10 heuristics (visibility of system status, match to real world, user control, consistency, error prevention, recognition over recall, flexibility, minimalist design, error recovery, help/documentation), including origin history and severity rating note.

**Cross-linked with:**
- `nielsens-heuristics-jagged-edge.md` — now points to this as the canonical definition source
- `ux-quality-rubric.md` — cross-links the usability dimension to this reference
- `evaluator-super-agent.md` — cross-links the heuristics as the framework underlying usability scoring
- `index.md` — added under Evaluation & Quality

**Connection to your active work:** This connects directly to the **evaluator-super-agent** (usability dimension scoring), **automated-usability-testing** (the heuristics are its rubric backbone), and was recently adopted as the team's base rubric at the July 1 design sync.

You might want to check out [UX Quality Rubric](~/second-brain/wiki/ux-quality-rubric.md) or [Evaluator Super Agent](~/second-brain/wiki/evaluator-super-agent.md) for how this is applied in your pipeline.
